coredata.dc:title: Continuous productions of highly fluorescent carbon dots and enriched polymer nanofibers via microfluidic techniques
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yifeng
Shen, Anze
Guo, Jiazhuang
Zhu, Liangliang
Li, Ge
Qin, Ying
Qu, Xiaowei
Wang, Cai-Feng
Chen, Su
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have shown great promise in various fields owing to their fascinating properties. However, most of high-performance CDs are usually synthesized upon intermittent operations, which are difficult to produce continuously in batch. In this regard, we develop a robust aluminum-based microfluidic chip reaction platform with imbibition-injection cycle feeding and ease of scale-up production, allowing the rapid and continuous synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs. The as-prepared CDs possess excellent optical properties, including high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of 71%, narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 65 nm by optimizing the parameters of reaction. Combined with electro-microfluidic spinning, the continuous production of fluorescent enriched CDs/polymer nanofibers could further be realized. CDs/thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) and CDs/polyamide 6,6 (PA66) nanofibers are prepared to exhibit enhanced mechanical properties with tensile strength 2.9 and 4.1 times higher than those of the pure polymer matrix, respectively. This work contributes a new kind of microfluidic chip for nanomaterial synthesis, and paves a way toward rapid and continuous preparation of high-performance CDs and CDs/polymers nanofiber films by microfluidic techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microfluidic chip
Continuous production
Fluorescence
Polymer nanofibers
Mechanical properties

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and construction of reaction equipment
Synthesis of CDs via aluminum-based microfluidic chip method
Preparation of CDs/polymers nanofiber films via electro-microfluidic spinning
Preparation of CDs/PA66 nanofiber films via electro-microfluidic spinning
Preparation of a blue LED lamp

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC2104600).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis process of high-performance CDs via aluminum-based microfluidic chip. (b) CDs/polymers nanofiber films by electro-microfluidic spinning for fiber reinforcement.

(a) Devices for the synthesis of CDs via aluminum-based microfluidic chip. Photographs of (b) an aluminum-based microfluidic chip, (c) CDs at the outlet of the chip under 365 nm UV irradiation, (d) CDs prepared with different residence times (21.6, 18.8, 15.4, 12.9, and 10.8 min) under daylight (top) and 365 nm UV irradiation (bottom), and (e, f) a batch of as-prepared CDs solution under (e) daylight and (f) 365 nm UV irradiation.

Fluorescence properties of CDs in DMF synthesized under different reaction conditions via aluminum-based microfluidic chip: (a, d) different reaction temperatures, (b, e) different reaction residence times in the microchannel at 180 °C, and (c, f) different reaction ratios of precursors at 180 °C (the amount of CA was 1 mmol, and that of Tris varied from 0, to 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mmol).

Optical properties of CDs in DMF. (a) Absorption spectrum, and normalized optimal excitation and emission spectra, respectively. Inset: Photographs of CDs under daylight and UV light irradiation. (b) PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence decay curve tested at optimal emission wavelength. (d) Photostability tests under the continuous irradiation of 365 nm UV light (3 W).

Morphology and structure of CDs. (a) TEM image (Insets: HRTEM image). (b) Particle size distributions. (c) XRD pattern. (d) Raman spectrum. (e) FT-IR spectrum. (f) XPS survey spectrum. High-resolution (g) C 1 s spectrum, (h) N 1 s spectrum and (i) O 1 s spectra.

(a, b) SEM images of CDs/TPU nanofiber. (c) Fluorescent images of CDs/TPU obtained using a laser scanning confocal microscope (excitation wavelength: 405 nm). (d, e) SEM images of CDs/PA66 nanofiber. (f) Fluorescent images of CDs/PA66 nanofiber taken by laser scanning confocal microscope under the 405 nm excitation light. (g) Normalized PL emission spectra and (h) PL decay curves of CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 nanofiber films.

(a) Strain-stress curves of CDs/TPU nanofiber films. (b) Strain-stress curves of CDs/PA66 nanofiber films. (c) Schematic diagram of mechanical strength enhancement mechanism of CDs/polymers nanofiber films.

head.ce:title: Continuous productions of highly fluorescent carbon dots and enriched polymer nanofibers via microfluidic techniques

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering and College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering and College of Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering and College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing 210009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have shown great promise in various fields owing to their fascinating properties. However, most of high-performance CDs are usually synthesized upon intermittent operations, which are difficult to produce continuously in batch. In this regard, we develop a robust aluminum-based microfluidic chip reaction platform with imbibition-injection cycle feeding and ease of scale-up production, allowing the rapid and continuous synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs. The as-prepared CDs possess excellent optical properties, including high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of 71%, narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 65 nm by optimizing the parameters of reaction. Combined with electro-microfluidic spinning, the continuous production of fluorescent enriched CDs/polymer nanofibers could further be realized. CDs/thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) and CDs/polyamide 6,6 (PA66) nanofibers are prepared to exhibit enhanced mechanical properties with tensile strength 2.9 and 4.1 times higher than those of the pure polymer matrix, respectively. This work contributes a new kind of microfluidic chip for nanomaterial synthesis, and paves a way toward rapid and continuous preparation of high-performance CDs and CDs/polymers nanofiber films by microfluidic techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a class of carbon-based nanomaterials with particle size less than 10 nm, carbon dots (CDs) are potentially useful in a variety of applications like optoelectronic devices [1,2], bioimaging [3,4], fluorescent detection [5,6], photothermal therapy [7,8], and catalysis [9,10], owing to their unique properties such as outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, and excellent water solubility [11,12]. Currently, numerous synthesis methods of CDs have emerged, including arc discharge method [13], laser sintering method [14], electrochemical synthesis [15], plasma method [16], solvothermal method [17,18], and microwave method [19,20]. Among those reported, hydro-/solvo-thermal treatments are most widely used to obtain CDs with favourable optical properties, wherein relatively lengthy time consumption (usually ∼24 h) and high pressure are required [18,21]. Process reinforcements assisted by microwave [22], magnetic hyperthermia [23], and plasma [16] have been reported to greatly speed the synthesis of CDs. Nevertheless, these processes are usually intermittent operations with low automation. Methods allowing the rapid and continuous synthesis of CDs with outstanding fluorescence properties and ease of scalable production are highly desirable to meet wide applications of CDs.

As a continuous operation technology, microfluidics has promising applications in the chemical synthesis of nanomaterials due to its precise regulation of fluids in microscale space [24,25]. The large specific surface area of the microchannels makes the mass and heat transfer rates more efficient [26,27]. Moreover, this continuous production approach has potential of industrial scale-up production [28,29]. In this respect, microfluidic chips are the key components that contribute to the building of microfluidic flow reactors for continuous production. Recently, researchers have constructed some microfluidic chips using tubular microreactors [30,31], modular low-temperature co-fired ceramic [32], foamy copper [33,34], PDMS [35] and other reactors [36] to achieve the synthesis of fluorescent CDs. Although researchers have made great progress, most of microfluidic chips still suffer from defects such as poor solvent tolerance, complex processing, poor thermal conductivity and heat resistance, which have potential risks in the synthetic process and limit their industrial-scale continuous production. Therefore, it is important to develop a robust controllable microfluidic chip platform to synthesize CDs with excellent optical properties.

Another project worthy of intensive study is the use of CDs as nanofillers for enhancing the performance of polymer materials like mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties, flame retardancy, and corrosion resistance [37,38]. For instance, some success has been achieved with the incorporation of CDs into polymers including plastics [39,40], rubbers [41,42], natural fibers [43] and gel polymers [44] to reinforce their mechanical performance. On the other hand, synthetic fibers with good mechanical properties have a significant role in our daily life as well as show great promise in intelligent textile-related applications [45,46]. In our previous works, we introduced CDs into artificial polymeric fibers such as polyacrylonitrile (PAN) or alcohol soluble polyurethane (APU) to obtain nanocomposites with improved mechanical strength along with other merits like fluorescence and biocompatibility [47]. Continuous production of various CDs-reinforced synthetic fibers via proper microfluidic techniques is highly desirable and requires further scientific endeavor.

Herein, we achieved the rapid and continuous synthesis of high-performance CDs as well as CDs-enriched polymer nanofibers via microfluidic techniques. We designed a robust aluminum-based microfluidic chip reaction platform with imbibition-injection cycle feeding (Fig. 1 a). Compared to other microfluidic chips, the aluminum-based microfluidic chip has better thermal conductivity, excellent sealing, efficient mass and heat transfer rate, etc. It also has advantages of great temperature resistance (above 600 °C), solvent corrosion resistance and low manufacturing costs, which makes it attractive for the synthesis of various materials. Specific examples were implemented on the continuous synthesis of CDs with good optical properties by using citric acid (CA), tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (Tris) as precursors and DMF as solvent. By optimizing parameters including reaction temperature, flow rate, solvents and the ratio of precursors, the as-prepared CDs were obtained with full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 65 nm and photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of 71%. This robust aluminum-based microfluidic chip platform enables continuous production of highly fluorescent CDs, which might provide guidance for other nanomaterials preparations. In addition, the continuous production of fluorescent thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) and polyamide 6,6 (PA66) nanofibers with enhanced mechanical properties were discussed by using CDs as fluorescent nanofillers (Fig. 1b).

Formamide (AR, 99%), 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP, greater than99.5%), tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (Tris, ≥ 99.5%), anhydrous citric acid (CA, ≥99.5%), and thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Polyamide 6,6 (PA66) was provided by Nanjing Janus Co., Ltd. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.5%), ethylene glycol (AR, ≥99.5%) and formic acid (≥88%, analytical reagent) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Diethylene glycol (CP, ≥98.0%), polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400, CP, ≥99.5%) and 1,2-propanediol (AR, ≥99.0%) were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and construction of reaction equipment
ce:section.ce:para: The aluminum-based microfluidic chip was fabricated on an aluminum substrate by computer numerical control (CNC) cutting technology. The parameters of microchannel of chip are as follows: the microchannel length is 450 mm, the channel depth is 1 mm, the circular and rectilinear microchannel diameters are 12 and 2.5 mm, respectively. Subsequently, the aluminum-based microfluidic chip reaction platform was built, mainly consisting of three parts: feeding, high-temperature reaction and product collection region. The aluminum-based microfluidic chip and bidirectional push–pull syringe pump (Nanjing Janus New-Materials Co., Ltd.) were connected by silicone tubes (3*4 mm), and the precursor solution was pumped into the chip for reaction through the bidirectional push–pull syringe pump, which could realize the cyclic feeding of precursor solution. Finally, the solution of CDs was collected in a conical flask at the outlet of the chip. Due to the large specific surface area of the internal microchannels, the aluminum-based microfluidic chip acted as a reaction zone, which could effectively improve mass and heat transfer rates. At the same time, the fluorescent properties of CDs could be precisely tuned by optimization of the reaction parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs via aluminum-based microfluidic chip method
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 10 mmol of CA and 2.5 mmol of Tris were firstly added to 10 mL of DMF and sonicated for 10 min to obtain a colorless solution. Then, the aluminum-based microfluidic chip was preheated to the set reaction temperature (140–200 °C) in an oil bath. After that, the precursor solutions were loaded into a 20 mL syringe and pumped into the chip via bidirectional push–pull syringe pump to prepare CDs. Subsequently, the CDs solutions could be collected at the outlet of the chip. The optical properties of CDs could be regulated by adjusting temperature, flow rate, solvents and the ratio of precursors. Importantly, the injection and imbibition syringes connected in the opposite direction rely on the liquid pressure generated by the push–pull forces to enable continuous supplement of the precursors and continuous feed of the precursor solution could be achieved just by reversing the direction of the syringe. Finally, the solution of CDs was cooled to room temperature, filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and treated by vacuum drying to provide the brown CDs powder directly used for fabricating CDs/polymers nanofiber films. The CDs samples for characterizations were further dialyzed (molecular weight cutoff (MWCO), 1000 Da) against deionized water (500 mL, changed twice a day) for 48 h to remove the unreacted reactants and organic molecules. Furthermore, we carried out the synthesis of the CDs by solvothermal treatment at 180 °C with the molar ratio of CA to Tris of 1:0.25 in DMF as a control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs/polymers nanofiber films via electro-microfluidic spinning
ce:section.ce:para: CDs/TPU nanofiber films were fabricated on an electro-microfluidic spinning machine (Nanjing Janus New-Materials Co., Ltd.). First, 1.4 g of TPU was dissolved in 8.6 g of DMF at room temperature to obtain a clear solution of 14 wt%. Meanwhile, CDs were dissolved in DMF by ultrasound to obtain different concentrations of CD solutions (2, 5, 8 and 10 wt%). Next, TPU and CDs solutions were sequentially loaded into two syringes and connected through a Y-shaped chip with a needle (19 G) as a spinneret. The distance between the needle and receiver was 10 cm. Then, the voltage and fluid flow rate were set to 18 kV and 0.5 mL/h, respectively. The voltage and liquid flow were set to 18 kV, 0.5 mL/h, respectively. Finally, CDs/TPU nanofiber films were collected on a tin foil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs/PA66 nanofiber films via electro-microfluidic spinning
ce:section.ce:para: CDs/PA66 nanofiber films were also fabricated via electro-microfluidic spinning. First, 1.5 g of PA66 was dissolved in 8.5 g of formic acid at room temperature to obtain a clear solution of 15 wt%. Meanwhile, CDs powders were dissolved in formic acid by ultrasound to obtain different concentrations of CDs solutions (2, 5, 8 and 10 wt%). Next, PA66 and CDs solutions were sequentially loaded into two syringes and connected through a Y-shaped chip with a needle (19 G) as a spinneret. The voltage and fluid flow rate were 16 kV, 0.5 mL/h, respectively. Finally, CDs/PA66 nanofiber films were collected on a tin foil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of a blue LED lamp
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs/TPU nanofiber film was frozen by liquid nitrogen and then ground into powder. Then the powder was mixed with optical glue and then coated on a 365 nm light chip. Finally, a dome-shaped protective cover was introduced as the outermost layer.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of CDs were measured on a PerkinElmer Lambda 900 UV–vis spectrometer. An Edinburgh FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer was employed to investigate the optical properties of CDs and CDs/polymer nanofibers including PL emission spectra, and time-resolved decay. The absolute PL QY of CDs was measured with further equipment of an integrating sphere on the fluorescence spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) for surface morphology of CDs was observed by using a FEI TECNAI G2 F20 microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs was measured by a FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet 6700). X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) was recorded on an ARL X’TRA powders X-ray diffractometer at room temperature with a scanning speed of 10° /min with 2θ range from 5° to 80°. Raman spectrum of CDs was obtained by Jobin Yvon Horiba LAB-RAM Infinity. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ES-CAIAB250 XPS system. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of fibers were observed by a HITACHI S-4800 scanning electron microscope. The fluorescence fields of nanofiber were recorded on Leica SP5 laser scanning confocal microscope under 405 nm excitation. The tensile experiments were measured using LF plus from LLOYD testing machine with the cross-head speed of 10 mm/min.

In this work, we developed continuous productions of highly fluorescent CDs and CDs-enriched polymer nanofibers via microfluidic techniques (Fig. 1). An aluminum-based microfluidic chip was initially designed and fabricated, based on which a microfluidic production platform was built with three main parts of feeding, reaction and product collection regions (Fig. 1a, Fig. 2 a–c, see details in experimental section). Fig. 1a shows an example for the continuous synthesis process of highly fluorescent CDs via this aluminum-based microfluidic chip production platform, which involves a continuous flow treatment at 140–200 °C. DMF was selected as the solvent for its low viscosity and excellent solubility for precursors. CA and Tris were selected as the carbon source and surface passivating agent [22,48], due to the abundant hydroxyl and amino functional groups which play an important role on the surface modification of the CDs. Importantly, the bidirectional push–pull injection pump was used to achieve continuous feeding of the precursor solution. By adjusting the flow rate, the residence time of the solution for carrying out reaction in the microchannel could be well controlled, to prepare CDs with easily tunable reaction time (Fig. 2d). In this way, we realized the safe and continuous synthesis of CDs with high PL QY of 71% which is comparable to those of other CDs previously developed with microfluidic chips (Table S1) [30,32–36]. As shown in Fig. 2e, f, the as-prepared CD solution is dark brown in daylight but shows bright blue fluorescence with the irradiation of a 365 nm UV light. This suggests the feasibility of applying aluminum-based microfluidic chip to synthesize high-performance CDs. The as-prepared CDs could be used as fluorescent nanofillers to enrich polymer nanofibers, and CDs/TPU and CDs/ PA66 nanofibers with enhanced mechanical properties and bright fluorescence were continuously produced via an electro-microfluidic spinning platform (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, both microfluidic platforms have scale-up capacities by numbering-up chips in parallel or by increasing operation time [25], and then scale-up productions of high-performance CDs and CDs-enriched polymer nanofibers could be available. With combination of the aluminum-based microfluidic chip reaction platform and the microfluidic spinning platform, synchronized continuous productions of CDs and CDs/polymer fibers could also be expected (Fig. S1). Nevertheless, both microfluidic platforms suffer from drawbacks commonly found in microfluidic systems, such as difficulty in cleaning and even sometimes chip blockage. And the aluminum-based microfluidic chip can’t resist strong acids and alkalis.

Here, we investigated the effect of different experimental conditions on the PL properties of CDs, including reaction temperature, residence time (flow rate) in the microchannel, and the ratio of precursors (Fig. 3 a–f, Table S2). Fig. 3a, d shows the reaction temperature has a great effect on optical properties of CDs. It can be found that either too high or too low reaction temperatures are not favorable for strongly fluorescent CDs, which may be caused by the insufficient or excessive chemical reaction in the microchannel. The fluorescence intensity of CDs reached the highest at 180 °C, which was finally determined as the optimal reaction temperature. On the other hand, the flow rate of precursors determines their reaction residence time in the microchannel and the properties of CDs can be precisely tuned. Therefore, we further investigated the effect of flow rate on the synthesis of CDs (10, 12, 15, 18, and 20 mL/h), corresponding to a channel residence time of 21.6, 18.8, 15.4, 12.9, and 10.8 min, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3b, e, PL intensity of CDs gradually increases along with a decrease in full width at half maximum (FWHM) as the residence time increases. The higher fluorescence properties are achieved at a residence time of 21.6 min. In addition, the variation of the molar ratio of precursors had a significant effect on the PL properties of CDs. The optical properties of CDs were studied in a series of experiments by fixing the amount of CA at 1 mmol and varying the addition of Tris (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 mmol), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3c, f, the addition of the appropriate amounts of Tris significantly improves the optical properties of CDs, which indicates that Tris as a passivator has a significant effect on the surface modification of CDs. Finally, the fluorescence intensity reached the highest at a CA to Tris ratio of 1:0.25.

Then we designed three experiments with different molar ratios of CA to Tris varying from 1: 0, to 1: 0.25 and 0: 1, and the corresponding product samples were named as CA-CDs, CDs and Tris-CDs, respectively. Both samples of CA-CDs and Tris-CDs show weak blue fluorescence with excitation-independent feature (Fig. S2), accompanied with the disappearance of absorption peak near 331 nm in their UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S3). This apparent difference suggests the dehydration polymerization between carboxyl and hydroxyl/amino groups between CA and Tris, followed by proper carbonization to form CDs. The doping of functional groups on CD surface should be an important reason for the bright blue fluorescence. The UV absorption spectrum were converted to Tauc plots of (αhν)2 versus hν to estimate the optional band gap (Eg), wherein α, h, and ν represent the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively [49,50]. The fitting results showed that the band gaps (E g) of CDs, CA-CDs, and Tris-CDs were 3.42, 4.08 and 4.11 eV, respectively (Fig. S4). During the excitation process of light, electrons (e-) start from the valence band (VB) and cross the forbidden band to the conduction band (CB), and are accompanied by the formation of holes (h + ) in VB. Electrons need external excitation energy to generate transition motion. The larger the bandgap, the greater the energy required for electrons to transition from VB to CB. Therefore, under the same conditions of photoexcitation, the doping of functional groups on the surface of CDs resulting in lower E g, the electrons and holes are more likely to undergo radiative recombination after photoexcitation, resulting in excellent fluorescence properties [51].

Relevant studies have found that the properties of solvents can play an important role in tuning the optical properties of CDs [52,53]. We also investigated the optical performance of CDs prepared with other high-boiling solvents for comparison, including ethylene glycol, formamide, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), diethylene glycol, Polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400), and 1,2-propanediol. Fig. S5 displays UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs prepared using different solvents, which have absorption peaks generally centred around 340 nm. From the PL spectra in Fig. S6, it is obvious that all the as-prepared CDs show blue fluorescence in those solvents. The PL characteristics of CDs synthesized in different solvents are summarized in Table S2. We found the CDs synthesized in DMF and ethylene glycol have stronger fluorescence, and those obtained in DMF have the highest PL QY. We speculated that may be related to the viscosity of solvents and flow state of solutions in the microchannel. As a good aprotic solvent, DMF has a relatively lower viscosity and a proper boiling point. It will decompose into gas at a certain degree under high reaction temperature, thus forming a gas–liquid segmented flow in the microchannels, along with its low viscosity, to make the reaction more uniform and effective with efficient heat transfer [31]. Meanwhile, this may facilitate the modification of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs resulting in strong fluorescence emission. Moreover, better solubility of CA and Tris in DMF could promote the mass transfer and a more efficient chemical reaction, allowing the preparation of high-performance CDs.

Based on preliminary experiments above, the effect of various reaction variables on the PL QY and FWHM of CDs, including reaction temperature, residence time (flow rate) in the microchannel, the ratio of precursors, and solvents was revealed, summarized in Table S2. It can be found that the changing trend of PL QY is consistent with the fluorescence intensity, and the PLQY and PL intensity of CDs increase along with a decrease in FWHM. As such, we picked out the optimal reaction parameters, namely reaction temperature (180 °C), microfluidic flow rate (10 mL/h, i.e. residence time of 21.6 min), molar ratio of CA to Tris (1:0.25), and proper solvent (DMF), to prepare high-performance CDs with good optical properties. The PL QY of the CDs prepared under optimized conditions reaches up to 70.98% along with a FWHM of 65 nm based on the robust microfluidic chip method, which is comparable to those previously obtained by hydrothermal treatment for 6 h [48]. Control experiments based on solvothermal treatment at 180 °C and high pressure were carried out, which could yield fluorescent products with PL QY of 69% at 50 min in this case (Fig. S7). The high mass and heat transfer properties of the aluminum-based microfluidic chip may promote the dehydration polymerization and carbonization processes, which made it possible to obtain high-performance CDs in a short time. Meanwhile, this rapid and continuous method has the potential of industrial scale-up of CDs with good optical properties. The mass yield of the CDs purified by filtering and dialysis was 28.3%, which means that with the merit of continuous production this method owns a production efficiency of 15.1 g/day, surpassing other conventional intermittent operations like the most commonly used hydro-/solvo-thermal methods usually with production efficiency lower than 0.2 g a batch [54,55]. Furthermore, by numbering-up chips in parallel, the mass production of high-performance CDs in this method can be expected.

The optical properties of CDs prepared under optimal conditions were thoroughly investigated. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared solution of CDs exhibits a shoulder peak at 331 nm (Fig. 4 a), which might be ascribed to n-π* transition of C = O or C = N [48]. Also, the optimal excitation wavelength of the CDs is 375 nm, which gives the PL emission peak centered at 421 nm. Obviously, CDs in DMF are colorless under daylight but emit strong blue fluorescence under UV light (Insets in Fig. 4a). We measured the PL emission spectra of CDs using different excitation wavelengths, and found that CDs have excitation-independent PL emission (Fig. 4b). The PL QY of CDs is as high as 70.98% at 375 nm excitation (Fig. S8). The fluorescence decay curve of CDs could be fitted with a single exponential decay function, offering a fluorescence lifetime of 10.72 ± 0.05 ns (Fig. 4c), indicating that the fluorescence of CDs mainly comes from the radiative decay, and with less energy loss released from nonradiative decay [54].

The photostability and thermal stability of CDs were investigated. The photostability tests for CDs were performed under continuous irradiation with a 365 nm UV light, and the results show that the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains above 91% after 100 min continuous irradiation (Fig. 4d). We also investigated the photostability of CDs in aqueous solutions with different pH values (1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0, 11.0 and 13.0) (Fig. S9). All samples except those with pH values of 11 and 13 show relatively high photostability, to preserve more than 90% of fluorescence intensity after 100 min of continuous irradiation. Nevertheless, the fluorescence intensity changes with pH, which implies that the fluorophores on the surface of CDs contribute to the fluorescence [56]. The thermal property of the CDs is also important for further practical applications of CDs. Then the fluorescence stability was investigated in the temperature range of 20 to 140 °C by heating the sample at a certain temperature for 100 min (Fig. S10). The fluorescence intensity of CDs after heating treatment was always maintained above 90%, which indicates good thermal stability of CDs.

We further analysed the morphology and chemical structure of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis reveals that the as-prepared CDs are well dispersed nanoparticles without obvious aggregation. These CDs have well-resolved lattice fringes with an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, indicating good crystallinity of CDs (Inset in Fig. 5a). The average particle size of CDs was determined to be 1.80 nm using particle size analysis software (Fig. 5b). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs shows a broad peak at 19.8° (Fig. 5c), corresponding to the (002) interlayer spacing of the graphite structure. A small peak is also found at 2θ = 40.6°, attributed to the d-spacing (100) crystal plane of graphene, corresponding to a lattice stripe spacing of 0.21 nm [17,57], which is consistent with the stripe spacing observed in the HRTEM image, indicating a high degree of graphitization. Two peaks found in Raman spectrum of CDs, centred at 1385 and 1588 cm−1 (Fig. 5d), are attributed to structural defects (D band) and graphite sp2 carbon atom (G band) [58,59]. This indicates that as-prepared CDs are mainly composed of sp3- or sp2- hybridized carbon. The ratio of D-peak intensity to G-peak intensity (ID/IG = 0.88) indicates a high degree of graphitization of CDs. The above results imply that CDs consist of nanocrystalline graphitic sp2 carbon core with sp3 carbon defects [23].

FT-IR spectrum was further carried out to verify the presence of abundant polar functional groups in CDs (Fig. 5e). The absorption peak at 3433 cm−1 is assigned to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The stretching vibration of C-H at 2926 cm−1, C = O stretching vibrations at 1626 cm−1, C = C stretching vibrations at 1561 cm−1 and C–N bending vibrations at 1414 cm−1 are observed. The strong absorption peak at 1124 cm−1 could be ascribed to the C–O stretching vibration [22,48]. These groups make CDs remarkably water-soluble. In order to further characterize the surface groups of CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed, as shown in Fig. 5f–i. The full survey spectrum shows peaks of C 1 s (284.79 eV), N 1 s (399.98 eV), and O 1 s (531.93 eV), suggesting that CDs are mainly composed of C, N, and O elements, with relative elemental contents of 65.1%, 0.6% and 34.2%, respectively (Fig. 5f). The high-resolution C 1 s reveals three peaks at 284.68 eV for C = C/C-C, 286.1 eV for C-N/C-O, and 288.69 eV for C = O (Fig. 5g). The N 1 s band reveals C = N at 399.75 eV and N-C = O at 401.37 eV (Fig. 5h). The O 1 s band reveals two peaks at 531.89 and 533.12 eV, ascribable to C = O and C-O, respectively (Fig. 5i). The results reveal the existence of rich functional groups on the surface of the CDs.

The formation and fluorescence origin of CDs were further discussed. The control experiments with and without addition of CA and Tris for optical characterizations confirm the contribution of both CA and Tris in the formation of CDs (Figs. S2–S4). Other optical, structural and compositional characterizations presented above indicate that CDs consist of nanocrystalline graphitic sp2 carbon core surface-functionalized with sp3 carbon defects and abundant functional groups, and imply the contribution of these rich functional groups on the CD surface as fluorophores for fluorescence emission. The abundant functional groups may also inhibit the nonradiative recombination of excitons, and enhance the π-conjugated system making the electron mobility enhanced and the electrons more easily excited, which are more favourable to enhance the fluorescence of CDs [60]. The dialysis process of the rude CD product with use of a dialysis bag with MWCO of 1000 Da leaded to the separation of main product CDs (in the dialysis bag) and organic molecules (out the dialysis bag). Some impurity of blue fluorescent molecules was found in the dialysate during the dialysis process (Fig. S11). These results indicate that the CDs fabricated in this work are composed of carbon core surface-functionalized with abundant molecular fluorophores which contribute to strong blue fluorescence [52,53].

We further developed continuous productions of highly fluorescent CDs-enriched polymer nanofibers via a microfluidic electrospinning technique. Artificial polymers such as TPU and PA66 are widely used in various applications [45,61], and the continuous fabrication of these polymers with both good optical and mechanical performance is highly desirable. Here we realized the continuous productions of CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 nanofibers via microfluidic electrospinning (Fig. 1b). The morphology of the as-prepared CDs/polymer nanofibers was characterized by SEM measurements. The surface of CDs/TPU (Fig. 6 a, b) and CDs/PA66 (Fig. 6d, e) nanofiber films are smooth. They exhibit the disordered arrangement under the combined effect of electric field force and coulomb force. These fibers have an average diameter size of 356 and 234 nm, respectively (Fig. S12). Bright fluorescence of CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 nanofiber films are founded by fluorescence confocal scanning microscopy under 405 nm excitation light, which strongly demonstrates the successful incorporation of CDs into the polymer matrix (Fig. 6c, f). As shown in Fig. S13, pure TPU and PA66 nanofiber films have very weak fluorescence, while CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 nanofiber films show strong PL emission. The fluorescence emission peaks of CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 nanofibers are centred at 430 nm and 419 nm at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, respectively (Fig. 6g). The absolute PL QYs of CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 are 54.47% and 64.61%, respectively. The results suggest these CDs with rich surface functional groups have good compatibility with polymers, allowing the availability of uniform CDs/polymer nanofibers with high fluorescence. The fluorescence decay lifetimes of CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 nanofiber films are 9.75 ns and 10.02 ns under the 405 nm excitation light, respectively (Fig. 6h). As shown in Fig. S14, the hydrophilicity of the CDs/polymer nanofiber films are enhanced with increasing of concentration of CDs, which may be due to the presence of abundant polar functional groups on the surface of CDs.

The incorporation of CDs into polymer fibers allows the availability of nanocomposites with enhanced mechanical properties. Fig. 7 a, b display strain–stress curves of CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 nanofiber films with different concentrations of CDs, respectively. When the mass fraction of CDs is 8 wt%, the tensile strength of CDs/TPU nanofiber film is up to 8.6 MPa, which is 2.9 times of that of pure TPU nanofiber film (3.0 MPa). Also, the elongation at break of CDs/TPU increases from 13.4% to 22.3%. Similarly, upon the incorporation of CDs into PA66 nanofiber films, the tensile strength increases from 3.9 to 16.1 MPa, with 4.1-time increasement. CDs/PA66 nanofiber film also exhibits excellent elongation at break, increasing from 14.6% to 28.4%. The effect of CDs on improving the mechanical properties of various materials is summarized in Table S3.

A possible mechanism for enhancing the mechanical properties of CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 nanofiber films is illustrated graphically in Fig. 7c. By precise control of microfluidic parameters, CDs can be uniformly distributed in the polymer matrix to form strong hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces. Abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, act as anchor points to connect CDs and polymer chains (between the amide groups on polymers and oxygen-containing groups on CDs) [47,62]. Under external tensile forces, the presence of hydrogen bonding makes the polymer molecular chains stiffer and harder to break, thus improving the mechanical strength of the nanofibers. However, the reinforcement effect of the CDs on the tensile strength of the TPU and PA66 nanofiber films starts to decrease when the CDs concentration is higher than 8%. Excessive incorporation of CDs might lead to the dominance of other interactions, such as π-π stacking interaction and van der Waals interaction, which would disrupt the balance between the carbon dots and the polymer, causing a decrease of the enhancement [63].

As such, based on the rapid and continuous synthesis of high-performance CDs with aluminum-based microfluidic chip platform, we further realized the continuous production of high-strength fluorescent nanofibers, which have potential applications in the field of optoelectronic devices. For example, we explored the potential of CDs/polymers nanofiber films for optoelectronic applications. Take the application of CDs/TPU nanofiber films for blue light conversion materials as an example. A CDs/TPU nanofiber film was ground into powder and coated on a 365 nm chip to construct a blue-emissive LED lamp (Fig. S15a). The device exhibits an emission peak at 420 nm (Fig. S15b) and Commission Internationable de L’Eclairage (CIE) 1931 chromaticity coordinates of (0.15, 0.04) (Fig. S15c). As shown in inset in Fig. S15b, the device emits bright blue light at turn-on state. The results demonstrate the success in the construction of blue LED lamp with use of these composite materials as phosphors.

In summary, this work contributes a new kind of microfluidic chip for nanomaterial synthesis, and paves a way toward rapid and continuous preparation of high-performance CDs and CDs/polymers nanofiber films by microfluidic techniques. We designed an aluminum-based microfluidic chip to achieve the rapid and continuous synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs. Benefiting from various advantages of aluminum-based microfluidic chip, such as great temperature resistance, lower manufacturing costs, better thermal conductivity and solvent tolerance, we synthesized CDs rapidly and continuously by using non-toxic precursors. By regulating the microfluidic reaction parameters, the obtained CDs exhibit outstanding fluorescent performance with high PL QY (70.98%), narrow FWHM (65 nm) and excellent optical stability. Furthermore, we developed continuous productions of highly fluorescent CDs-enriched polymer nanofibers with enhanced mechanical properties via a microfluidic electrospinning technique, including CDs/TPU and CDs/PA66 nanofibers CDs, wherein CDs act as fluorescent nanofillers. The tensile strength and elongation at break of CDs/TPU nanofiber films are 2.9 and 1.7 times those of pure TPU, and the CDs/PA66 nanofiber films are 4.1 and 1.9 times those of pure PA66, respectively. This work successfully achieved the rapid and continuous preparation of CDs and CDs-enriched polymer nanofibers by microfluidic techniques, and is expected to be useful to guide the synthesis of other nanomaterials and nanocomposites. Furthermore, the embedding of CDs as nanofillers into the synthesized nanofibers can further improve the mechanical properties, which has a wide prospect in the field of optoelectronic applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC2104600).
